tracker_summary.inventor: Simon Campbell, David Roberts, and research team at Pfizer Sandwich, UK
tracker_summary.current_use: Treatment of erectile dysfunction and pulmonary arterial hypertension
tracker_summary.initial_funding_reason: Developing a treatment for angina pectoris and hypertension

the_x: Pfizer's cardiovascular drug research program targeting angina and hypertension
the_y: First oral treatment for erectile dysfunction, generating over $1.9 billion annually at peak
summary: Pfizer chemists set out to find a new angina drug by inhibiting PDE5 to relax coronary blood vessels. The compound UK-92480 showed modest cardiovascular effects but produced a striking unreported side effect in male patients. Rather than abandon the compound, Pfizer physician Ian Osterloh championed investigating the side effect, pivoting the entire program to erectile dysfunction. The result was Viagra, approved in 1998 — 2.9 million prescriptions in the first three months.

chain.what_they_were_doing: Researching cyclic GMP signaling pathways as a mechanism for relaxing smooth muscle in blood vessels to treat angina and hypertension.
chain.funding: Pfizer Inc. internal R&D, estimated $150M+ across the cardiovascular program.

chain.who: Simon Campbell and David Roberts (medicinal chemists)
chain.what_they_were_doing: Designing pyrazolopyrimidinone compounds to inhibit PDE5, which breaks down cGMP — the molecule that signals blood vessel relaxation.
Synthesizing and screening candidates; UK-92480 (sildenafil citrate) identified as lead compound with selective PDE5 inhibition.

chain.where: Multiple trial sites, UK and Wales (including Morriston Hospital, Swansea)
chain.what_they_were_doing: Phase I and Phase II trials for angina and hypertension. Results were disappointing — modest blood pressure reduction and limited antianginal effect.
chain.the_jump: Male trial participants were reluctant to return unused pills. Nurses noted an unusual pattern of reported erections as a side effect — initially underdocumented in formal reports.

chain.what_they_were_doing: Reviewing failed cardiovascular trial data and the informal side-effect reports from Morriston Hospital nurses and patients.
chain.the_jump: NEAR-DEATH MOMENT: Osterloh made the decision not to terminate UK-92480, instead proposing that the erectile side effect be formally investigated. This reversed the program's trajectory entirely. Without Osterloh's advocacy, the compound would have been shelved.

chain.where: Trial sites across USA, Europe, and Brazil
chain.what_they_were_doing: 21 double-blind placebo-controlled trials enrolling over 3,000 men for erectile dysfunction. Demonstrated efficacy and acceptable safety.
FDA approved Viagra on March 27, 1998 — the first oral pill for erectile dysfunction. 2.9 million prescriptions in the first three months. Peak annual sales exceeded $1.9 billion.
chain.the_jump: A failed angina drug became one of the most commercially successful pharmaceuticals in history, and later found a second life treating pulmonary arterial hypertension (as Revatio).

sources: Osterloh IH. 'The discovery and development of Viagra.' https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3731515/
Boolell M, et al. 'Sildenafil, a novel effective oral therapy for male erectile dysfunction.' BJU. 1996;78(2):257-261. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/8813947/
Goldstein I, et al. 'Oral sildenafil in the treatment of erectile dysfunction.' NEJM. 1998;338(20):1397-1404. https://www.nejm.org/doi/full/10.1056/NEJM199805143382001
Campbell SF. 'Science, art and drug discovery: a personal perspective.' Clinical Science. 2000;99(4):255-260. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/10995571/
FDA Drug Approval History for Sildenafil. https://www.accessdata.fda.gov/scripts/cder/daf/index.cfm?event=overview.process&ApplNo=020895
